items.prompt_md: In the LCS table, when `a[i-1] == b[j-1]`, what is `dp[i][j]`?
options: 0, because a match resets the count
items.explanation_md: A match means this element belongs in the LCS, so you extend the LCS of the prefixes *before* both matched elements by one.

items.prompt_md: You're given two strings and asked for the length of their longest common subsequence. Which approach fits?
options: Sort both strings and compare character by character
Build a 2-D table dp[i][j] over prefixes of both strings, branching on match vs. no-match
Slide a fixed-size window across the longer string
Use a hash map counting each character's frequency
items.answer: Build a 2-D table dp[i][j] over prefixes of both strings, branching on match vs. no-match
items.explanation_md: Comparing two sequences element by element with a match / no-match branch is the fingerprint of two-sequence DP — a 2-D table indexed by "how far into each sequence."

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of filling the LCS table for strings of length m and n?
items.explanation_md: Every one of the (m+1)*(n+1) cells is computed once from a constant number of neighboring cells, giving O(m*n) time and space.
